name: The plot and the bargain
notes: All three synoptists set the stage for the passion before the last supper, in two movements: the chief priests' plot and Judas's bargain to betray. The shared core of the plot is the nearing feast of the passover and unleavened bread, the chief priests and scribes seeking how to take Jesus and kill him, and their dread of the people. Matthew alone fixes the timing ('after two days') and names the palace of Caiaphas, splitting the assembly (26:3) from the consultation (26:4), while Luke compresses the council's caution into 'they feared the people' as a complementary vantage on Matthew and Mark's 'Not on the feast day, lest there be an uproar.' In the bargain, all three record Judas, one of the twelve, going to the chief priests, their gladness and covenant of money, and his seeking opportunity to betray him; Matthew alone names the thirty pieces of silver, Luke alone tells that Satan entered into Judas and that he sought the absence of the multitude, and Mark notes Judas would do it 'conveniently' — each evangelist preserving his own detail.
